claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 2 children:
  - Alex is 4 years old and does not live with you.
  - Blake is 14 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 4 (under 16)
  - Claimant contributes £50.00/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 14 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 4 (under 16); Claimant contributes £50.00/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Claimant contributes £50.00/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Blake
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 14 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment for you.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Let's begin the Child Benefit eligibility assessment.

**First, I need to know about the child you're claiming for:**

How old is the child (or children) you want to claim Child Benefit for?

- **Under 16 years old**
- **16 to 19 years old**
- **20 years old or over**

Please let me know their age, and if you have multiple children, please tell me the ages of each child you'd like to claim for.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hi, thanks for helping me with this. I have two children I'd like to ask about - Alex who's 4 years old, and Blake who's 14.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! It's great to meet you. Let me help you check eligibility for both Alex (4 years old) and Blake (14 years old).

Since both of your children are **under 16 years old**, they automatically meet the age requirement for Child Benefit. Now I need to check a few more things for each of them.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
The next question is about whether the children are working:

**Are Alex and Blake both working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work at all)?**

Given their ages (4 and 14), I'd assume neither is working full-time, but please confirm this is the case.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: That's right, neither of them are working at all. Alex is only 4, and Blake is still in school.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: That makes perfect sense! Since neither Alex nor Blake are working, they meet this requirement. Let me continue with the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question: Are Alex and Blake NOT receiving any benefits in their own right?**

This means they are not personally claiming benefits such as:
- Employment and Support Allowance (ESA)
- Universal Credit

Given their ages, this is very unlikely, but I just need to confirm that neither child is receiving these benefits in their own name.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, neither of them receive any benefits in their own name.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great, thank you for confirming. Let me continue to the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question: Are Alex and Blake in any apprenticeships?**

Most apprenticeships disqualify a child from Child Benefit, with the exception of Foundation Apprenticeships in Wales.

Given that Alex is 4 and Blake is 14 and still in school, I assume neither is in an apprenticeship. Is that correct?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, that's correct - neither of them are in any apprenticeships.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect, thank you. Now let's move on to check some requirements about you as the claimant.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Now I need to check your residency status.**

To be eligible for Child Benefit, you need to meet one of the following residency requirements. You qualify if **any one** of these applies to you:

1. **You normally live in the UK**
2. **You have settled status** under the EU Settlement Scheme
3. **You have pre-settled status** and meet certain conditions (working, seeking work, studying, have sufficient resources, or are a family member of an EEA/Swiss national)
4. **You're a Crown servant** living abroad

Do any of these apply to you? For most people, the first option (normally living in the UK) is the most common.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK - I'm in Manchester.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! Since you normally live in the UK, you meet the residency requirement. Let me continue with the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next, I need to confirm your responsibility for Alex and Blake.**

You're considered responsible for a child if **any one** of these applies:

1. **You live with the child**
2. **You contribute to their upkeep** (at least the equivalent of Child Benefit towards their care - such as food, clothes, pocket money, housing)
3. **You pay maintenance** for the child under a court order or agreement

Do Alex and Blake both live with you, or do you contribute to their care in one of these ways?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Blake lives with me at home, but Alex doesn't live with me. However, I do help out with Alex - I contribute towards things like clothes and school dinners.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying. So for Blake, you live together, and for Alex, you contribute to their upkeep. Both of these can qualify you as being responsible for the children.

Just to confirm for Alex - are your contributions towards their care (clothes, school dinners, etc.) at least equivalent to the Child Benefit amount? This is currently around £25.60 per week for a first child or £16.95 for additional children. Your contributions can be weekly, monthly, or even lump sum payments.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I contribute £50 a week towards Alex's upkeep.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: That's great - £50 per week is well above the Child Benefit threshold, so you clearly meet the responsibility requirement for both children. Let me continue with the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Important question: Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex or Blake?**

Child Benefit can only be paid to one person per child. Here are the possibilities:

- **No one else is claiming** for either child
- **Someone else is claiming**, but you've agreed between yourselves who should receive it
- **There's a dispute** about who should claim

Do you know if anyone else is currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex or Blake?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for either of them.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great, thank you for confirming. Let me continue to the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Now I need to check the living situation for both children.**

Are Alex and Blake currently in any of the following situations?

- **In hospital** for an extended stay
- **In local authority care**
- **In a fostering arrangement**
- **Living normally** (at home, with family, or in regular accommodation)

From what you've told me, it sounds like Blake lives with you at home. What about Alex - where do they normally live?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Blake lives with me at home, and Alex lives with my ex-partner. Both are just living normally - neither is in hospital, care, or being fostered.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for clarifying. Both children are living normally, so let me continue with the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Final question: Is the local council NOT paying towards either Alex's or Blake's accommodation or maintenance?**

You generally cannot receive Child Benefit if the local council is paying for the child's accommodation or upkeep.

Is this the case for both children - that the council is not paying for their accommodation or maintenance?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: That's correct - the council isn't paying anything towards either of them.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Wonderful! That was the final check. Based on all the information you've provided, I can now give you the outcome.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
---

## 🎉 Great News - You're Eligible for Child Benefit!

Based on the information you've provided, **you are eligible to claim Child Benefit for both Alex and Blake**.

### Summary:

| Child | Age | Eligibility |
|-------|-----|-------------|
| **Alex** | 4 | ✅ Eligible |
| **Blake** | 14 | ✅ Eligible |

### How Both Children Qualify:

**Alex (age 4):** Although Alex lives with your ex-partner, you qualify because you contribute £50 per week towards their upkeep, which is well above the Child Benefit threshold.

**Blake (age 14):** Blake lives with you, so you clearly meet the responsibility requirement.

Both children are under 16, not working, not receiving benefits, and not in any disqualifying situations.

---

### Next Steps to Claim:

1. **Apply online or by post** using form CH2
2. **Provide proof of identity** for yourself and both children
3. **Provide birth certificates** for Alex and Blake

### Important Information:

- **Payments** are usually made every 4 weeks (or weekly if you're a single parent or receiving certain benefits)
- ⚠️ **High Income Child Benefit Charge:** If you or your partner earns over £60,000 per year, you may need to pay some or all of the benefit back through tax

### Useful Link:
🔗 [How to claim Child Benefit](https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit/how-to-claim)

---

Is there anything else you'd like to know about Child Benefit or the claims process?

reasonings: Alex (age 4) meets all eligibility criteria: (1) Under 16 years old - automatically qualifies on age, (2) Not working, (3) Not receiving benefits in their own right, (4) Not in an apprenticeship, (5) Claimant normally lives in the UK (Manchester), (6) Claimant is responsible for Alex by contributing £50 per week towards their upkeep (clothes, school dinners), which exceeds the Child Benefit threshold, (7) No other person is claiming Child Benefit for Alex, (8) Alex is living normally with the claimant's ex-partner, and (9) The local council is not paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance.

Blake (age 14) meets all eligibility criteria: (1) Under 16 years old - automatically qualifies on age, (2) Not working (still in school), (3) Not receiving benefits in their own right, (4) Not in an apprenticeship, (5) Claimant normally lives in the UK (Manchester), (6) Claimant is responsible for Blake as they live together, (7) No other person is claiming Child Benefit for Blake, (8) Blake is living normally at home with the claimant, and (9) The local council is not paying towards Blake's accommodation or maintenance.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant is eligible to claim Child Benefit for both children. They meet all residency requirements (living in Manchester, UK), and are responsible for both children - living with Blake and contributing £50 per week towards Alex's upkeep. Both children are under 16, not working, not receiving benefits, not in apprenticeships, and the local council is not contributing to either child's care. No one else is currently claiming Child Benefit for either child.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

Working and earning/expect to earn above primary National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months

Looking for work (can claim as jobseeker for up to 91 days)
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Has sufficient resources to support self and family financially
NOT claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Studying and has sufficient resources to support self financially

paths.description: Pre-settled status as family member of EEA/Swiss national
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Is family member of EEA or Swiss national with right to reside
One of: spouse/civil partner, dependent child/grandchild under 21, dependent parent/grandparent

paths.requirement: Living abroad as Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to responsibility check

Contributions include: food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents
Can be weekly, monthly, or lump sum payments

criteria: Payments made towards child's maintenance under court order or agreement
Amount equals or exceeds Child Benefit payment

response.description: Check if another person is claiming Child Benefit for this child

response.description: Determine if special rules apply based on child's location

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex (age 4) meets all eligibility criteria: (1) Under 16 years old - automatically qualifies on age, (2) Not working, (3) Not receiving benefits in their own right, (4) Not in an apprenticeship, (5) Claimant normally lives in the UK (Manchester), (6) Claimant is responsible for Alex by contributing £50 per week towards their upkeep (clothes, school dinners), which exceeds the Child Benefit threshold, (7) No other person is claiming Child Benefit for Alex, (8) Alex is living normally with the claimant's ex-partner, and (9) The local council is not paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance.

Blake (age 14) meets all eligibility criteria: (1) Under 16 years old - automatically qualifies on age, (2) Not working (still in school), (3) Not receiving benefits in their own right, (4) Not in an apprenticeship, (5) Claimant normally lives in the UK (Manchester), (6) Claimant is responsible for Blake as they live together, (7) No other person is claiming Child Benefit for Blake, (8) Blake is living normally at home with the claimant, and (9) The local council is not paying towards Blake's accommodation or maintenance.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant is eligible to claim Child Benefit for both children. They meet all residency requirements (living in Manchester, UK), and are responsible for both children - living with Blake and contributing £50 per week towards Alex's upkeep. Both children are under 16, not working, not receiving benefits, not in apprenticeships, and the local council is not contributing to either child's care. No one else is currently claiming Child Benefit for either child.
